company.name: Kenvue

marketCap.rationale: Kenvue's IPO was priced at $22 per share and reported as valuing the company at about $41 billion on its first trading day; this is treated as the IPO-era equity valuation.

company.description: Kenvue sells consumer health products across self-care, skin health and beauty, and essential health brands.

overview.title: Consumer health portfolio
paragraphs: Kenvue is a large pure-play consumer health company with 2025 net sales of about $15.1 billion across self care, skin health and beauty, and essential health brands.

Its portfolio includes Tylenol, Neutrogena, Aveeno, Band-Aid, Listerine, Nicorette, Johnson's, and Zyrtec, with products reaching consumers through pharmacies, mass retail, grocery, e-commerce, and professional recommendation channels.

overview.title: Registry relevance
paragraphs: Kenvue is relevant to the registry because its products sit at the edge of brand trust, regulated consumer health, commodity chemistry, retail distribution, and repeat household purchasing.

The strongest decentralization pressure is not a direct open-source clone of Kenvue's full portfolio. It is pressure from generics, transparent labels, independent batch verification, cooperative procurement, refill infrastructure, reusable packaging, and local production for simpler non-drug personal care categories.

moatNarrative: Kenvue's moat comes from trusted brands, healthcare-professional credibility, retail shelf access, formulation know-how, regulatory compliance systems, and the difficulty of earning consumer trust in pain relief, skincare, baby care, wound care, and oral care.

The moat is strongest where safety and credibility matter, such as Tylenol's acetaminophen franchise and Neutrogena's dermatologist-developed skincare positioning. It is weaker where the underlying chemistry is generic or where consumer behavior can shift toward refill, transparency, or lower-cost private-label alternatives.
decentralizationNarrative: Kenvue is a low-to-moderate decentralization target because most products are physical consumables with safety, quality, labeling, manufacturing, and distribution constraints. OTC drugs and sunscreen-adjacent products are especially hard to decentralize honestly because incorrect dosing, contamination, or false claims can create real harm.

Credible openings still exist. Generic acetaminophen already weakens brand exclusivity, while open regulatory data, DailyMed labeling, FDA safety materials, cosmetics product-listing requirements, open biomaterials databases, and local recycling projects can support more transparent procurement, refill, reuse, and batch-verification networks.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 251-275; rankApprox is set to the cohort midpoint because no more granular rank was provided in the intake.

moat.rationale: Kenvue owns many high-recognition consumer health brands, serves a large global user base, operates in trust-sensitive categories, and benefits from retail distribution, professional recommendation, and compliance scale.

decentralizability.rationale: Physical health and beauty products face formulation, safety, labeling, manufacturing, and liability barriers, but generics, transparent ingredient data, cooperative procurement, refill systems, and local packaging loops create partial decentralization paths.

profitability.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported 2025 revenue of about $15.12 billion and 2025 EBIT of about $1.99 billion, indicating a profitable but not unusually high-margin consumer staples business.

peRatio.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Kenvue's trailing P/E ratio at about 21.3 as of June 2026; this is a point-in-time market metric and can move daily.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap reported Kenvue's market capitalization at about $35.34 billion as of June 2026, with an end-of-day June 23, 2026 figure from CompaniesMarketCap of about $35.34 billion.

products.name: Tylenol
products.category: OTC pain relief and fever reduction
products.summary: Tylenol is Kenvue's acetaminophen-based over-the-counter pain relief and fever reduction brand, spanning adult, children, infant, cold, flu, sleep, and topical pain formats.
products.whyItMatters: Tylenol is a trust-sensitive consumer health brand where dosing safety, active-ingredient clarity, professional recommendation, retail availability, and brand memory all reinforce the incumbent position.
replacementSketch: A realistic replacement path would not be informal home production of acetaminophen. It would start with compliant generic acetaminophen, transparent labels, independent batch checks, and cooperative purchasing channels that make trusted low-cost supply easier to compare with branded Tylenol.

The hard parts are regulatory compliance, dose accuracy, child-safety packaging, recalls, adverse-event handling, and consumer trust when a medication is used for pain, fever, pregnancy questions, and pediatric care.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated generic acetaminophen verification
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federation of pharmacies, clinics, consumer co-ops, and independent labs could coordinate compliant generic acetaminophen purchasing while publishing label references, lot metadata, assay results, and adverse-event routing so trust shifts from a single branded label toward auditable generic supply.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure changes if brand trust is replaced in part by transparent generic procurement, lot-level verification, and shared quality records that let buyers compare safe acetaminophen products without relying on Tylenol's brand premium.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Bitcoin is not central. Decentralization matters through federated verification, cooperative purchasing, and independent quality records that can be operated by many pharmacies, clinics, labs, and buyer groups without one branded consumer-health company controlling the trust layer.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Participating buyers pool demand, contract with compliant manufacturers or distributors, reference DailyMed and FDA materials for labeling, and publish batch records plus test results in a shared registry. Local pharmacies or clinics handle fulfillment and consumer counseling.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Trust depends on regulated supply channels, NDC and label matching, tamper-evident packaging, independent potency and contaminant testing, recall monitoring, and adverse-event reporting. Cheating is constrained by lot-level audits and pharmacy accountability, but the system still depends on regulated manufacturers and credible labs.
failureModes: Small buyer groups may not save enough money to overcome the convenience and familiarity of branded Tylenol.
Poorly governed verification could create false confidence in counterfeit, expired, misdosed, or contaminated medicine.
Regulatory, liability, packaging, and pediatric dosing obligations may keep the model confined to narrow pharmacy or clinic channels.
adoptionPath: Start with adult single-ingredient acetaminophen supplied through pharmacies, clinics, or employer health programs where procurement and counseling are already structured.
Add independent lot testing, public label links, recall monitoring, and clear dosing education before expanding into pediatric liquid products or combination cold and flu medicines.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept decentralizes trust, purchasing, and verification, but the underlying drug manufacturing and distribution must remain tightly regulated.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: DailyMed, openFDA, pharmacy procurement, and independent labs provide plausible coordination primitives, but creating a durable consumer-facing federation would require governance and liability discipline.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Generic acetaminophen supply exists, but safe pediatric dosing, recalls, counterfeit resistance, adverse-event handling, and lab economics make broad decentralized implementation difficult.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The model can pressure price transparency and generic substitution, but it is unlikely to displace Tylenol's brand trust quickly in high-anxiety household medicine use.

products.name: Neutrogena
products.summary: Neutrogena is Kenvue's dermatologist-developed skincare and beauty brand, spanning cleansers, moisturizers, acne care, sunscreens, makeup, body care, hair care, and skin analysis tools.
products.whyItMatters: Neutrogena shows how a consumer health company turns formulation, dermatologist credibility, retail placement, ingredient narratives, and habitual routines into a defensible beauty and skincare franchise.
replacementSketch: A credible decentralized wedge would begin with simpler non-drug skincare categories such as cleansers, moisturizers, and body care, where refill shops, transparent ingredient lists, reusable packaging, and local batch records can matter without making drug-like claims.

The model gets harder around sunscreen, medicated acne treatments, eye-area products, preservatives, contamination control, fragrance allergens, and claims that trigger stronger regulatory or clinical expectations.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open skincare refill and batch-trust network
disruptionConcepts.summary: A network of refill shops, cosmetic chemists, local producers, and consumer co-ops could publish formula envelopes, ingredient sourcing, safety-substantiation records, batch IDs, and reusable packaging standards for simple skincare products, competing with Neutrogena on transparency and local trust rather than celebrity-scale brand advertising.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market changes if consumers can buy verified, locally fulfilled skincare staples with clear ingredients, reusable packaging, and batch accountability instead of defaulting to national branded bottles for every routine product.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Bitcoin is not central. Decentralization matters through local manufacturing, cooperative ownership, open ingredient documentation, reusable packaging loops, and many independently operated refill or production nodes.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Chemists define safe formula ranges, local producers make narrow product lines, refill shops manage reusable containers, and consumer co-ops pool demand. Shared batch records and product-listing practices keep the network interoperable across locations.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Verification relies on ingredient disclosure, batch IDs, product listing, safety substantiation records, sanitation logs, preservative testing, container wash records, and customer-visible recall procedures. Cheating is constrained by local reputation and random testing, but contamination and exaggerated claims remain serious risks.
failureModes: Small operators may struggle to meet cosmetic safety, preservation, sanitation, and recordkeeping requirements consistently.
Consumers may prefer Neutrogena's dermatologist-developed positioning and familiar retail availability for face products.
The model is poorly suited to sunscreen, medicated acne treatments, and other products where regulatory claims and clinical performance are harder to verify locally.
adoptionPath: Start with non-drug cleansers, body wash, basic moisturizers, and refillable containers in co-ops, zero-waste retailers, campuses, or dense neighborhoods.
Add shared testing protocols, safety-substantiation records, allergen disclosures, reusable packaging standards, and batch recall playbooks before expanding to more sensitive product types.

decentralizationFit.rationale: Simple skincare and packaging loops can be locally produced and refill-oriented, but many Neutrogena categories remain constrained by safety, claims, and quality-control requirements.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open biomaterials data, reusable packaging practices, and local recycling projects provide coordination primitives, but a credible skincare QA network would need stronger chemistry and safety governance.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: Basic non-drug skincare and refill systems are feasible, while preservation, contamination control, fragrance allergens, product listing, and consumer claims keep implementation materially harder than generic household refills.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept can pressure packaging waste, ingredient transparency, and refill expectations, but Neutrogena's brand trust, dermatologist positioning, and retail distribution remain hard to displace broadly.
